trajectory_summary: The call built energy cleanly from an already-engaged customer and landed a Saturday appointment — a genuine win on a warm lead. The rep's core problem is not energy management but qualification discipline: the decision-maker structure was never resolved against the codex standard, and the bill anchor was captured but not flipped. The booking is real but structurally exposed if the parents are needed to sign.
outcome_dispute: The call outcome is a booked in-home appointment for Saturday. The v1 tag is unknown, but the outcome is a sit — not a callback, not a tentative. The fragility is not in the booking itself but in the unresolved title-holder question, which is a handoff risk, not an outcome reclassification.

primary_coaching_focus.topic: Adult-child decision-maker protocol — title holders must be present at the appointment
primary_coaching_focus.quote: Yeah. So it's gonna be under my my mom and dad's name. I live here with them, so the the entire program will be under their name.
primary_coaching_focus.why: The rep heard the ownership disclosure, confirmed it ('So your parents are the homeowners. Correct?'), and then accepted Brandon's 'just put mine for now' without applying protocols.adult_child_decision_maker. The Saturday appointment is booked without confirming the parents will be present. If the closer arrives and needs a title-holder signature, the sit is at risk. This is the single most consequential gap in the call — it's a paycheck-protection issue, not just a coaching note.

key_moments.quote: Yeah. So it's gonna be under my my mom and dad's name. I live here with them, so the the entire program will be under their name.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon is the adult child, not the homeowner of record. The codex is explicit: co-decision-makers on the title must be present at the appointment. The rep acknowledged the disclosure but did not apply the protocol — no attempt to confirm whether the parents would be on the Saturday call.

key_moments.quote: Just just put mine for now if that's okay. Okay. Let me proceed from there.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the moment the protocol failure crystallized. Brandon is explicitly asking the rep to proceed under his name despite having just disclosed the title is in his parents' names. The rep said 'K' and moved on. The codex requires the rep to ensure title holders are present at the appointment — accepting 'just put mine for now' bypasses that requirement entirely.

The rep captured the kWh number efficiently off a paper bill — good pivot given the mail-bill situation. But per coaching_philosophy.bill_anchoring, capture alone is not sufficient. The pattern is 'ask + acknowledge + flip.' The rep got the number and moved on to residence length. No emotional alignment against the utility, no curiosity created around what that usage means in dollars. The bill anchor was collected as data, not deployed as leverage.

key_moments.quote: I have a Tesla phone. I have a Tesla, so it's gonna be a little bit more.
key_moments.stewart_read: Brandon volunteers that he has a Tesla (vehicle) — an unsolicited signal that his electric load is higher than the 1348 kWh baseline suggests. This is a qualification-strengthening detail: EV ownership means higher ongoing load, which improves the solar economics and system sizing case. The rep said 'Okay. Okay.' and moved on. The signal was heard but not anchored.
